dominant_error_pattern: Student underestimated anxiety disorder prevalence (thinking they are rare) and denied school impact, missing the fact that 10-15% of children and significant academic/social consequences require intervention.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A and D both minimize anxiety prevalence and impact. Teach prevalence statistics in context: 1 in 6-7 children affected, ~50% of school refusals are anxiety-driven, academic decline accelerates untreated. Show screener examples (SCARED, GAD-7) and map anxiety severity to functional impairment: mild (still attends) → severe (school refusal).

dominant_error_pattern: Student conflated ADHD inattention and impulsivity deficits with autism's core social communication and restricted-repetitive behavior deficits, missing the diagnostic boundaries.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A pairs impulsivity (ADHD marker) with social obliviousness (ASD marker) as if interchangeable. Teach the diagnostic profile: ASD → social reciprocity + restricted interests + repetitive movements (present in case); ADHD → inattention/hyperactivity/impulsivity (not present). Use a comparison table: ASD child with intense train obsession + flapping vs. ADHD child with restless energy but flexible interests.

dominant_error_pattern: Student prescribed medication or punishment when inattentive ADHD requires tier 1 classroom-based environmental scaffolding (organization supports, clear directions, movement breaks) as first intervention.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A and C bypass environmental intervention entirely. Teach MTSS tier logic: Tier 1 (universal design: visual schedules, folder systems, graphic organizers) → Tier 2 (small group check-ins) → Tier 3 (1:1 behavioral plan) → medication as adjunct. Show how a visual task checklist and timer eliminate 80% of initiation failures before medication is considered.

dominant_error_pattern: Student normalized anxiety as attention-seeking or believed avoidance (removing student from demands) would resolve it, missing that accommodation reinforces anxiety cycles.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A and C both use avoidance logic: removing triggers (trains anxiety) or dismissing as attention-seeking (minimizes urgency). Teach the anxiety maintenance cycle: trigger → anxiety → avoidance → temporary relief → stronger anxiety next time. Show how school reintegration with coping skills (breathing, self-talk) + gradual exposure breaks the cycle, while excusal strengthens it.

dominant_error_pattern: Student treated depression and ADHD as mutually exclusive conditions rather than clinically common comorbidities that require simultaneous assessment and treatment planning.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A incorrectly separates the disorders. Teach prevalence of comorbidity: ~18% of ADHD children also have depression, requiring dual-axis treatment. Create a case: student with ADHD restlessness + depressive anhedonia (lost interest in soccer). Show how treating ADHD alone misses mood symptoms; ADHD intervention (structure + rewards) can improve mood baseline, but depression may need additional support.

dominant_error_pattern: Student overgeneralized from oppositional arguing (ODD hallmark) to Conduct Disorder severity (rights violations, aggression to property/animals/people), missing the severity threshold and violation-of-rights criterion.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor B inflates ODD arguing to theft/property destruction. Teach a continuum: arguing/refusing (ODD) → aggression/destruction (Conduct Disorder) → violence/theft (more severe CD). Use the stem's six-month arguing pattern as ODD-sufficient; clarify that theft/deceitfulness would trigger CD consideration instead.
